AGENT-AUTH.
Specification Implementations Examples Registry GitHub

Examples

End-to-end flows

Practical examples showing how Agent Auth works from registration to authenticated requests.

Registration

First-time agent registration with device flow

When a host (e.g. Cursor) creates an agent for the first time on a server, the server triggers a device authorization flow. The user approves in their browser, and the agent is created.

1
host server
Host sends agent creation request
Includes host public key, agent public key, requested capabilities.
POST /agent/register { publicKey, hostKey, name, capabilities }
2
server host
Server triggers device authorization flow
Returns verification URL and device code. User must approve.
→ { device_code, user_code, verification_uri }
3
user server
User approves host and agent
User can trust the host (auto-approve future agents) or approve this agent only.
4
host server
Host polls, receives agent ID
Host is registered. Agent is created. On future requests, the host is recognized.
→ { agentId, hostId, capabilities: [...] }
Authenticated Request

Cryptographically signed agent request

Every agent request carries a short-lived JWT (60s TTL) with DPoP-style request binding. The server verifies the signature, checks the binding, and resolves the agent's capabilities.

JWT payload
{
  "sub": "agt_k7x9m2...",       // agent ID
  "iat": 1709736000,            // issued at
  "exp": 1709736060,            // expires in 60s
  "jti": "unique-request-id",   // replay protection
  "htm": "POST",                // HTTP method binding
  "htu": "/api/reports",        // HTTP URI binding
  "ath": "sha256(body)"         // body hash binding
}
HTTP request
POST /api/reports HTTP/1.1
Host: api.example.com
Authorization: Bearer eyJhbGciOiJFZERTQSIsInR5cCI6...
Content-Type: application/json

{ "query": "monthly summary" }
Scope Escalation

Requesting additional capabilities at runtime

Capabilities are separated from agent identity. When an agent needs more access, it requests "pending" capability grants. Users approve or deny each one independently.

Escalation flow
1. Agent requests additional capabilities
POST /agent/request-scope { capabilities: ["github.push", "slack.send"] }
2. Server creates "pending" capability grants, returns verification URL
→ { requestId, status: "pending", verificationUrl }
3. User approves or denies each capability independently
POST /agent/approve-scope { requestId, action: "approve", capabilities: ["github.push"] }
Server Verification

Verifying agent requests on the server

On the server, extract the JWT, verify the signature against the agent's stored public key, check request binding, and resolve the agent's active capabilities.

With Better Auth
const session = await auth.api.getAgentSession({ headers });

if (session) {
  const {"{"} agent, user {"}"} = session;{"
"}  // agent.id        → "agt_k7x9m2..."{"
"}  // agent.capabilities → [{"{"}id, status, grantedBy{"}"}]{"
"}  // user.id         → "user_xyz"{"
"}{"}"}
Raw protocol
1. Extract JWT from Authorization header
2. Look up agent by sub claim
3. Verify Ed25519 signature with stored public key
4. Verify request binding (htm, htu, ath)
5. Check JTI replay, expiry, lifecycle state
6. Resolve active capabilities from database
7. Return AgentSession { agent, capabilities, user }
GitHub / Specification / Discord
Built on Better Auth
